content: This systematic review synthesised the available evidence on the health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting (IF), drawing on Cochrane-protocol definitions and meta-analytic reviews. Formal taxonomy distinguishes alternate-day fasting (24-hour complete fasting alternating with ad libitum feeding), time-restricted feeding (≥12-hour daily fast), and 5:2 periodic fasting; a pooled meta-analysis of 28 trials distributes evidence across these three protocols (k=7 TRE, k=10 ADF, k=11 5:2) over 2–52-week durations [2]. Across reviews examining intermittent energy restriction versus continuous energy restriction in healthy adults and Muslim adults observing Ramadan fasting, efficacy evidence is directional for body composition and cardiometabolic biomarker improvement but does not demonstrate superiority over matched-energy continuous restriction [3]. Network meta-analyses applying RCT-only eligibility filters and effect-size ranges such as SMD 0.15 (95% CI 0.02–0.28) for analogous lifestyle-intervention endpoints illustrate small-but-significant magnitudes with narrow clinical translation [5][6]. Safety data on vulnerable populations were not routed into the formal evidence set, representing an evidence-synthesis gap. Primary clinical endpoints for definitive cardiovascular-prevention claims — cardiovascular mortality, myocardial infarction, and heart failure [1] — have not yet been established for IF. Current evidence supports IF as a viable dietary-protocol family without demonstrated incremental clinical benefit over continuous energy restriction.
